active_paradigms.idea: Replace ad-hoc time term with autonomous secondary-nucleation master-equation form (Meisl/Knowles) using monomer depletion m0-based capacity and inhibitor-suppressed autocatalysis
active_paradigms.description: Build an autonomous RHS with NO explicit time x0, since amyloid kinetics are self-consistent in the state c. Use free monomer f = plateau - c (mass conservation, capacity=c0-x4). Primary nucleation: kn = c1^2*x1 (monomer m0 dependent, small constant offset c2^2). Autocatalytic secondary nucleation: k2 = c3^2*x4*(c4^2 + c5^2*x2) capturing that new aggregate mass grows proportional to existing mass x4 times available surface seeded by M0. Total elongation-driving rate: rate = (kn + k2). Inhibitor suppresses secondary nucleation surface catalysis specifically: divide the secondary part by inhibitor_scale = 1 + c6^2*x3 + c7^2*x3*x4 (surface-blocking, concentration-dependent). Final expression = f*(kn + k2/inhibitor_scale). This removes the physically dubious -c8*t*c decay term, keeping the ODE autonomous so held-out curve timing extrapolates via the same dynamics rather than an explicit clock. All constants squared for positivity; f>=0 near plateau keeps rate bounded and smooth. Use 8 constants total.
active_paradigms.what_to_optimize: heldout_curve_shape_loss (onset/crossing timing) and heldout_curve_nmse; autonomy should improve extrapolation robustness across the 192 curves
active_paradigms.cautions: Ensure plateau c0 initializes >1 (try initial 1.2) so capacity f stays positive over normalized c in [0,1]; keep secondary term proportional to x4 so lag emerges naturally at c near 0; verify inhibitor divides ONLY the secondary term, not primary, so cd=0 reduces cleanly. Squared constants avoid negative rates.

active_paradigms.idea: Model inhibitor as an explicit lag-time extender via a Hill-type onset gate on autocatalytic growth to directly target crossing-timing shape loss
active_paradigms.description: The shape loss emphasizes delayed inhibitor curves and worst-curve timing. Molecularly, inhibitors extend the lag phase by sequestering monomer / capping fibril ends. Capture this with a smooth self-accelerating (autocatalytic) term whose effective onset is pushed later as cd rises. Let capacity = c0 - x4 (mass conservation). Let growth = (c1^2*x1 + c2^2*x2 + c3^2*x4*x1) representing primary + seed + secondary(monomer-fed) contributions. Introduce a smooth inhibitor delay factor delay = 1/(1 + c4^2*x3*(c5^2 + (1-x4))) so that at early times (small x4) inhibition is strongest (blocks onset), and it relaxes as aggregation proceeds — this stretches the lag without changing the plateau. Final expression = capacity*growth*delay. This is smooth, globally defined (denominator always >=1), autonomous, and directly reshapes onset timing which is what the high-quantile worst-curve shape term penalizes. Use 6 constants for reliable fitting on all datasets.
active_paradigms.what_to_optimize: heldout_curve_shape_loss specifically at the 10% and 25% crossing points (lag phase); maintain nmse via correct plateau
active_paradigms.cautions: Denominator 1 + c4^2*x3*(...) is always >=1 so no singularity. Keep (1-x4) bounded since normalized c in [0,1]. Do not multiply primary nucleation by delay too aggressively or cd=0 curves also slow — check that x3=0 makes delay=1 exactly. Initialize c0 near 1.1 for positive capacity.

active_paradigms.idea: Competitive-inhibition Michaelis-Menten kinetics where inhibitor raises the effective monomer half-saturation, coupled to logistic growth
active_paradigms.description: Treat fibril elongation as an enzyme-like saturating process: growth rate saturates in available monomer with a half-saturation constant that the inhibitor competitively increases (classic competitive inhibition: Km_eff = Km*(1 + I/Ki)). Define free monomer proxy m = c0 - x4 (mass conservation capacity). Effective saturation: sat = m / (c1^2 + m + c2^2*x3) where c2^2*x3 is the competitive inhibitor term raising the apparent Km (slows growth, especially at low remaining monomer / late stage, stretching approach to plateau). Autocatalytic driver: driver = c3^2 + c4^2*x4*(c5^2 + c6^2*x2) capturing primary offset plus secondary nucleation proportional to existing mass and seed M0, plus monomer input c7^2*x1. Final expression = (c3^2 + c4^2*x4 + c7^2*x1)*(c5^2 + c6^2*x2)*sat, i.e. a saturable autocatalytic rate. This gives sigmoidal onset, bounded growth (sat<=1 and m->0 at plateau), and mechanistically grounded competitive inhibition that alters both rate and timing. Smooth everywhere since denominator c1^2 + m + c2^2*x3 > 0. Keep ~8 constants.
active_paradigms.what_to_optimize: balance nmse (saturation gives correct plateau approach) and shape_loss (competitive Km shift reshapes late-phase and onset timing); robustness across cd range
active_paradigms.cautions: Ensure denominator strictly positive by keeping c1^2 offset (init ~0.3). At x3=0 sat reduces to standard Michaelis form preserving cd=0 behavior. Avoid making m negative by initializing c0>1 (try 1.1); if odeint overshoots plateau, sat*driver near m~0 -> 0 naturally damps it. Watch total constant count stays under 13.
